body:
This looks more like we have something longrunning which happens only once and is only measured once with one of the modules.

The original initial messsage contains this:

```shell
[TRACE] - (starship::context): Building HashSets of directory files, folders and extensions took 6.115897257s
```

Unfortunately, I've no idea what this is, but hopefully has an idea.

I discovered just a bit later that a few instances ran with interval about one second will be done at the single moment

I think systemd automount cause this 6 second delay. Starship doing some call and locks until systemd returns. That's the question - what the call is and how to set timeout to this call

I'll try to strace calls later

There is the longest call: `statx(AT_FDCWD, "/mnt/pc", AT_STATX_SYNC_AS_STAT, STATX_ALL, 0x7ffef293a800) = -1 ENODEV (No such device)`
Output of strace ```c execve("/usr/bin/starship", ["starship", "timings"], 0x7ffcbf7f7b98 /* 63 vars */) = 0 brk(NULL) = 0x559642f64000 access("/etc/ld.so.preload", R_OK) = -1 ENOENT (No such file or I experienced the same issue today after adding automounted nfs shares to my machine and(!) symlinking them into my home directory. Now with the network unavailable starship would take 90s to display the prompt, which not-so-coincidentally is systemd's timeout for the mount in question. I could trace it back to the `pijul_channel` module. At first I believed the problem is here: https://github.com/starship/starship/blob/7a544589a45932d2a412a4ed1a391438bb1137ec/src/modules/pijul_channel.rs#L12 Where the module builds the directory search context before checking if the module is even enabled, traversing the symlinked nfs mount.

However, the easy fix was actually already suggested in the documentation: 

https://starship.rs/config/#prompt
> If you have symlinks to networked filesystems, consider setting follow_symlinks to false.

So, setting this solved this issue for me. :+1:

*EDIT*: Except it didn't. When testing this I had removed the symlink to make my shell not hang all the time. It was only after I wrote this comment that I noticed I tested without the symlink :(
It would be nice solution, except it will not work if fs mounted directly in home, without symlinks

Why is starship recursively traversing home directory in the first place?
I have systemd automount autofs on ~/Music that is backed by nfs share. If the nfs server is down, starship prompt takes 75 seconds to render (default timeout for mounting). 

Disabling `python` module is a workaround. `follow_symlinks = false` does not help.

> Why is starship recursively traversing home directory in the first place?

It seems to even be accessing drives on `/mnt`. Which if anything, I would expect default behavior to not follow symbolic links here. I'm not sure auto following, and thus auto mounting, is the best default behavior. 

People automount drives to save time as well as electricity costs. I believe this goes counter to the byline `The minimal, blazing-fast ... shell`. At worst this should be asynchronous and non-blocking. 

I also suspect that this may be due to something other than symlink following. At least as reported by `ls`, automounts are not symbolic links. Does `starship` march through directories? 

I don't know enough rust but isn't that what's happening [here](https://github.com/starship/starship/blob/master/src/context.rs#L496)? So this is a longshot, but the code looks similar to that in [PathBuf's read_dir](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/path/struct.PathBuf.html#method.read_dir) which is an alias for [fs::read_dir](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/fs/fn.read_dir.html) which runs [std::fs::DirEntry](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/fs/struct.DirEntry.html) where there's a note

> On Unix, the DirEntry struct contains an internal reference to the open directory. Holding DirEntry objects will consume a file handle even after the ReadDir iterator is dropped.

If I understand this correctly then this access the directory and thus triggers the automount, right? Not being a rust dev idk if I am misreading or way off base but I looked in case it could provide some help.

`ls`ing a directory won't cause the mount (e.g. `ls /mnt/myautomount` Note that this returns nothing when not mounted) but `cd`ing into it will.

> `ls`ing a directory won't cause the mount

AFAIK, it will

I'am not sure if it fully limits ascending searches for all modules, but `GIT_CEILING_DIRECTORIES` can be used to avoid scanning past specific directory boundaries for the git repo discovery.

That said the cwd `scan_timeout` behaviour should be improved.

After some tracing, I've also found my config to have created delays of 
approximately **2 minutes** with the log
```
[DEBUG] - (starship::config): Config found for "python": Some(Table({"symbol": String("\u{e235} "), "pyenv_version_name": Boolean(true), "format": String("[[ $symbol ($version) ](fg:#769ff0 bg:#212736)]($style)")}))
[TRACE] - (starship::context): Building HashSets of directory files, folders and extensions took 72.837677839s
[TRACE] - (starship::modules): Took 72.837816406s to compute module "python"
```
from what I initially thought was an issue with NodeJS/Python. This was easily 
reproducible when opening a new terminal was failing to resolve and running 
the trace in an already running shell.
Then I found this issue and tried reading the code and had to unmount 
everything to stop it from hashing everything on my /mnt/*network_share*.
I'm not sure how much more I can help contribute into debugging this.
